title: Ownership in Rust
content:
### Ownership in Rust

Ownership is Rust's most unique feature and the foundation of its memory safety guarantees without a garbage collector.

---

### Ownership Rules

| Rule | Description |
|------|-------------|
| Each value has exactly one owner | The variable that owns the value |
| Ownership can be transferred | Assignment or function calls can move ownership |
| Owner goes out of scope, value dropped | Memory is freed automatically |

---

### Move Semantics

```rust
let s1 = String::from("hello");
let s2 = s1;  // s1 is MOVED to s2
// println!("{}", s1);  // ERROR: s1 is no longer valid

// For Copy types, assignment copies instead
let x = 5;
let y = x;  // x is i32 (Copy), both are valid
println!("{}", x);  // OK
```

Types that implement the `Copy` trait are copied on assignment instead of moved. Scalar types and tuples of Copy types are Copy.

> **Key Point:** In Rust, only one variable owns a value at any time. When ownership is transferred (moved), the original owner can no longer use the value. This eliminates dangling pointers and double-free errors at compile time.

quiz.question: What happens when ownership of a value is moved?
options: The original owner can no longer use the value
